title: A progressive wave is represented by the equation $y = 0.2\sin\left(2\pi\left(10t - 0.1x\right)\right)$, where $x$ and $y$ are in meters and $t$ is in seconds. The wave velocity is ________ $\text{m/s}$.
solution_text: The standard wave equation is $y = A\sin(\omega t - kx)$. Comparing this with the given equation: $\omega = 2\pi \times 10 = 20\pi\,\text{rad/s}$, and $k = 2\pi \times 0.1 = 0.2\pi\,\text{rad/m}$. The wave velocity is $v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{20\pi}{0.2\pi} = 100\,\text{m/s}$.
common_mistake: Using wave speed $v = \omega \cdot k$ or forgetting to multiply by the $2\pi$ factor outside the parentheses.

title: The tension in a stretched string is increased by $21\%$. The percentage change in the speed of the transverse wave in the string is:
notes: Wave speed $v = \sqrt{T/\mu}$. Write the ratio of new speed to old speed.
solution_text: The velocity of a transverse wave in a string is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$, where $T$ is the tension and $\mu$ is the mass per unit length. If the tension is increased by $21\%$, the new tension is $T' = 1.21 T$. The new velocity is $v' = \sqrt{\frac{1.21 T}{\mu}} = 1.1 \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} = 1.1 v$. The percentage change in speed is $\frac{v' - v}{v} \times 100 = \frac{1.1v - v}{v} \times 100 = 10\%$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using linear approximation $\Delta v / v = \frac{1}{2} \Delta T / T = 10.5\%$. The linear approximation is only valid for very small percentage changes (like $1\%-2\%$) and fails for $21\%$.

title: Two coherent sound sources of intensities $I_1$ and $I_2$ interfere to produce a maximum intensity $I_{\text{max}}$ and a minimum intensity $I_{\text{min}}$. If $I_1 / I_2 = 9$, then the ratio $I_{\text{max}} / I_{\text{min}}$ is:
notes: Intensity ratio is related to amplitude ratio: $I_{\text{max}}/I_{\text{min}} = \left(\frac{\sqrt{I_1} + \sqrt{I_2}}{\sqrt{I_1} - \sqrt{I_2}}\right)^2$.
solution_text: Given $I_1 / I_2 = 9 \implies \sqrt{I_1/I_2} = 3$. The ratio of maximum to minimum intensity is $I_{\text{max}}/I_{\text{min}} = \left(\frac{\sqrt{I_1} + \sqrt{I_2}}{\sqrt{I_1} - \sqrt{I_2}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{\sqrt{I_1/I_2} + 1}{\sqrt{I_1/I_2} - 1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{3+1}{3-1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{2}\right)^2 = 4$. Thus, the ratio is $4:1$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming $I_{\text{max}}/I_{\text{min}} = I_1 / I_2 = 9$, forgetting that intensities add as amplitudes square during coherent interference.

title: A string of length $1.2\,\text{m}$ fixed at both ends vibrates in its third harmonic. The distance between two adjacent nodes in this vibration is ________ $\text{cm}$.
solution_text: For a string fixed at both ends, the condition for standing waves is $L = n\frac{\lambda}{2}$. In the third harmonic ($n=3$): $L = 3\frac{\lambda}{2} \implies \lambda = \frac{2L}{3} = \frac{2 \times 1.2}{3} = 0.8\,\text{m} = 80\,\text{cm}$. The distance between two adjacent nodes is $\frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{80\,\text{cm}}{2} = 40\,\text{cm}$.
common_mistake: Reporting the wavelength $\lambda = 80\,\text{cm}$ as the node spacing, or using $n=4$ for the third harmonic.

title: The ratio of the speed of sound in Hydrogen gas ($H_2$, molar mass $2\,\text{g/mol}$) to that in Helium gas ($He$, molar mass $4\,\text{g/mol}$) at the same temperature is:
notes: The speed of sound in a gas is $v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma RT}{M}}$. Note that $H_2$ is diatomic ($\gamma = 7/5 = 1.4$) and $He$ is monatomic ($\gamma = 5/3 \approx 1.67$).
solution_text: The speed of sound in a gas is $v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma RT}{M}}$. At the same temperature: $\frac{v_{H_2}}{v_{He}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma_{H_2}}{M_{H_2}} \frac{M_{He}}{\gamma_{He}}}$. For Hydrogen ($H_2$, diatomic): $\gamma_{H_2} = 7/5$, $M_{H_2} = 2$. For Helium ($He$, monatomic): $\gamma_{He} = 5/3$, $M_{He} = 4$. Substituting: $\frac{v_{H_2}}{v_{He}} = \sqrt{\frac{7/5}{2} \times \frac{4}{5/3}} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{12}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{84}{50}} = \sqrt{\frac{42}{25}}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Neglecting the difference in $\gamma$ (assuming speed only depends on molar mass $M$, which yields $\sqrt{2}$).

title: An open organ pipe of length $L_o$ and a closed organ pipe of length $L_c$ have the same fundamental frequency. The ratio of their lengths $L_o / L_c$ is:
notes: Fundamental frequency of open pipe is $f_o = v/(2L_o)$. For closed pipe, $f_c = v/(4L_c)$.
solution_text: Fundamental frequency of an open organ pipe: $f_o = \frac{v}{2L_o}$. Fundamental frequency of a closed organ pipe: $f_c = \frac{v}{4L_c}$. Given $f_o = f_c \implies \frac{v}{2L_o} = \frac{v}{4L_c} \implies 2L_o = 4L_c \implies L_o / L_c = 2$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming they have the same length ratio of $1:1$, or using the wrong denominator factors (mixing 2 and 4).

title: Two tuning forks A and B produce 5 beats per second. When B is slightly loaded with wax, the beat frequency becomes 3 beats per second. If the frequency of A is $340\,\text{Hz}$, the frequency of B is ________ $\text{Hz}$.
solution_text: Since A and B produce 5 beats/s: $f_B = f_A \pm 5 = 340 \pm 5 \implies f_B = 345\,\text{Hz}$ or $335\,\text{Hz}$. Loading B with wax decreases its frequency ($f_B' < f_B$). If $f_B = 345\,\text{Hz}$, then loading it with wax reduces it, say to $343\,\text{Hz}$, so the new beat frequency with A ($340\,\text{Hz}$) is $343 - 340 = 3$ beats/s. This matches the problem statement. If $f_B$ was $335\,\text{Hz}$, loading it with wax would reduce it to say $333\,\text{Hz}$, so the beat frequency would become $340 - 333 = 7$ beats/s (which is an increase). Hence, the original frequency of B must be $345\,\text{Hz}$.
common_mistake: Reporting $335\,\text{Hz}$ because they didn't test the loading effect carefully, or adding wax increases frequency.

title: A car sounding a horn of frequency $400\,\text{Hz}$ moves with speed $20\,\text{m/s}$ towards a stationary vertical wall. If the speed of sound in air is $320\,\text{m/s}$, the frequency of the echo heard by the driver of the car is ________ $\text{Hz}$.
solution_text: 1) The wall acts as a stationary observer receiving frequency $f_w = f_0 \left(\frac{v}{v - v_s}\right) = 400 \left(\frac{320}{320 - 20}\right) = 400 \left(\frac{320}{300}\right) = \frac{1280}{3}\,\text{Hz}$. 2) The wall then reflects this frequency, acting as a stationary source. The moving driver acts as an observer moving towards the wall with speed $v_o = 20\,\text{m/s}$. The frequency heard by the driver is $f' = f_w \left(\frac{v + v_o}{v}\right) = \left(\frac{1280}{3}\right) \left(\frac{320 + 20}{320}\right) = \left(\frac{1280}{3}\right) \left(\frac{340}{320}\right) = 4 \times \frac{340}{3} = \frac{1360}{3} \approx 453.33\,\text{Hz}$. Rounding to the nearest integer gives 453.
common_mistake: Using a single-stage Doppler formula, or subtracting $v_o$ in the numerator, yielding $400\,\text{Hz}$.

title: A standing wave in a stretched string fixed at both ends is represented by $y = 5\sin(kx)\cos(100\pi t)\,\text{mm}$, where $x$ is in meters and $t$ is in seconds. If the distance between two successive nodes is $10\,\text{cm}$, the maximum speed of a particle at $x = 2.5\,\text{cm}$ is:
notes: Distance between successive nodes is $\lambda/2 = 10\,\text{cm} = 0.1\,\text{m} \implies \lambda = 0.2\,\text{m} \implies k = 2\pi/\lambda = 10\pi\,\text{rad/m}$. Velocity of particle is $v_y = \frac{\partial y}{\partial t}$.
solution_text: Successive node distance $\lambda/2 = 10\,\text{cm} = 0.1\,\text{m} \implies \lambda = 0.2\,\text{m}$. Wave number $k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = 10\pi\,\text{rad/m}$. Particle velocity $v_y = \frac{\partial y}{\partial t} = -5\sin(10\pi x) \cdot 100\pi\sin(100\pi t)\,\text{mm/s}$. The maximum particle speed is $v_{\text{max}} = 500\pi \sin(10\pi x)\,\text{mm/s}$. At $x = 2.5\,\text{cm} = 0.025\,\text{m}$: $10\pi x = 0.25\pi = \pi/4$. Thus, $v_{\text{max}} = 500\pi \sin(\pi/4) = \frac{500\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = 250\sqrt{2}\pi\,\text{mm/s} = 0.25\sqrt{2}\pi\,\text{m/s}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Confusing wave velocity ($v = \omega/k$) with particle velocity ($v_y = \partial y/\partial t$).

title: In a resonance column experiment, the first resonance is obtained at length $18\,\text{cm}$ and the second resonance is obtained at length $56\,\text{cm}$ using a tuning fork of frequency $500\,\text{Hz}$. The speed of sound in air calculated from this data is ________ $\text{m/s}$.
solution_text: For a resonance column (closed at one end): $l_1 + e = \frac{\lambda}{4}$ and $l_2 + e = \frac{3\lambda}{4}$. Subtracting these two equations: $l_2 - l_1 = \frac{\lambda}{2} \implies \lambda = 2(l_2 - l_1) = 2(56 - 18) = 2(38) = 76\,\text{cm} = 0.76\,\text{m}$. The speed of sound is $v = f \lambda = 500 \times 0.76 = 380\,\text{m/s}$.
common_mistake: Calculating wavelength as $\lambda = 4 l_1 = 72\,\text{cm}$, neglecting the end correction $e$. Use subtraction to eliminate $e$.

title: The intensity of a sound wave is directly proportional to the square of its:
notes: Intensity of a wave is $I = 2\pi^2 \rho v f^2 A^2$. So $I \propto A^2$ and $I \propto f^2$.
solution_text: The intensity $I$ of a wave is the average energy transmitted per unit area per unit time. The formula for intensity is $I = 2\pi^2 \rho v f^2 A^2$, where $\rho$ is the density of the medium, $v$ is the wave velocity, $f$ is the frequency, and $A$ is the amplitude of the wave. Thus, the intensity is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude ($A^2$) and the square of the frequency ($f^2$). Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking intensity is proportional to amplitude rather than amplitude squared.

title: A uniform heavy rope of mass $M$ and length $L$ hangs vertically from a rigid support. A transverse wave pulse is produced at the lower end. The time taken by the pulse to travel to the top support is:
notes: Tension at distance $x$ from the bottom is $T(x) = (M/L)xg$. Wave speed is $v(x) = \sqrt{T/\mu} = \sqrt{xg}$. Write $dt = dx / v(x)$ and integrate from $0$ to $L$.
solution_text: Let $x$ be the distance from the bottom free end. The tension at this point is due to the weight of the rope below it: $T(x) = \mu x g$, where $\mu = M/L$ is the linear mass density. The speed of the wave pulse at position $x$ is $v(x) = \sqrt{\frac{T(x)}{\mu}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu x g}{\mu}} = \sqrt{xg}$. We know $v = \frac{dx}{dt} \implies dt = \frac{dx}{\sqrt{xg}}$. Integrating from $t=0$ to $t=t_0$ and $x=0$ to $x=L$: $t_0 = \int_0^L \frac{dx}{\sqrt{g}x^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{g}} [2x^{1/2}]_0^L = 2\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Using a constant acceleration model without integration, or using $v = \sqrt{Lg}$ as constant speed (yielding time $t = \sqrt{L/g}$).

title: An open organ pipe of length $L$ is in resonance with its second harmonic. If one end of the pipe is closed, the pipe is found to be in resonance with its $n^{\text{th}}$ harmonic with the same frequency. The value of $n$ is:
notes: Frequency of open pipe 2nd harmonic: $f_o = 2 \left(\frac{v}{2L}\right) = \frac{v}{L}$. Frequency of closed pipe $n^{\text{th}}$ harmonic: $f_c = n \left(\frac{v}{4L}\right)$ where $n$ must be an odd integer.
solution_text: For the open pipe, the frequency of the second harmonic is $f_{\text{open}} = 2 \left(\frac{v}{2L}\right) = \frac{v}{L}$. When one end of the pipe is closed, it becomes a closed organ pipe of the same length $L$. The resonance frequencies of a closed pipe are $f_{\text{closed}} = n \left(\frac{v}{4L}\right)$, where $n$ must be an odd integer ($n = 1, 3, 5, \dots$). For the frequencies to be the same: $f_{\text{open}} = f_{\text{closed}} \implies \frac{v}{L} = n\frac{v}{4L} \implies n = 4$. Since $n = 4$ is an even integer, and a closed organ pipe only supports odd harmonics, no such harmonic exists. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Simply setting $n=4$ without checking if even harmonics are supported in a closed organ pipe.

title: A stationary observer receives a sound of frequency $f_0$ from a source moving away with constant speed $v_s$. If a wind is blowing from the source to the observer with speed $w$, the observed frequency will be:
notes: When wind blows, the speed of sound relative to the ground becomes $v + w$ in the direction of wind.
solution_text: Since the wind is blowing from the source to the observer, the effective speed of sound in this direction is $v' = v + w$. The source is moving away from the observer (who is stationary) with speed $v_s$. By the Doppler formula: $f' = f_0 \left(\frac{v'}{v' + v_s}\right) = f_0 \left(\frac{v + w}{v + w + v_s}\right)$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Neglecting the wind velocity term in the numerator or denominator, or subtracting wind velocity.

title: An open organ pipe of length $L = 30\,\text{cm}$ and inner radius $r = 3\,\text{cm}$ vibrates in its fundamental mode. The wavelength of the sound wave emitted is ________ $\text{cm}$ (include end correction $e = 0.6r$ at each open end).
solution_text: An open organ pipe has two open ends, so end correction is applied at both ends: $L_{\text{eff}} = L + 2e = L + 2(0.6r) = L + 1.2r$. Given $L = 30\,\text{cm}$ and $r = 3\,\text{cm}$: $L_{\text{eff}} = 30 + 1.2(3) = 30 + 3.6 = 33.6\,\text{cm}$. For the fundamental mode of an open pipe: $L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\lambda}{2} \implies \lambda = 2 L_{\text{eff}} = 2 \times 33.6 = 67.2\,\text{cm}$.
common_mistake: Using only one end correction factor (like a closed pipe), or forgetting to double $L_{\text{eff}}$ to find the wavelength.
